sources.name: TASVideos — Genesis / Ristar
sources.notes: Speedrun documentation. Grab-cancel trick, bounce-boost trick, pole swing acceleration, Shooting Star Mode (6+ revolutions).

notes: DEFINING MECHANIC: Ristar does not have a standard attack — he extends his arms to GRAB enemies and objects, then headbutts them.
Ristar cannot damage enemies by contact — must grab and deliver the headbutt.
Pole swinging provides acceleration — multiple revolutions increase speed, eventually entering 'Shooting Star Mode' after 6 revolutions.
Physics are similar to Sonic 1 (same developer, same era) but the grab system creates completely different gameplay.
256 sub-pixel system likely (Sega Genesis Sonic Team standard), but Ristar-specific values not fully documented in reviewed sources.
Ristar is one of the most mechanically distinctive Sega Genesis platformers despite commercial underperformance.

subpixel_system.description: Likely 256 sub-pixels (Sega Genesis Sonic Team standard). Needs ROM-level verification specific to Ristar.

walk_speed.notes: Approximate from gameplay observation. Ristar is not a speed-focused character — movement is deliberate. VERIFY.

ground_movement.notes: Ristar's ground movement is less central to gameplay than the grab system. Most level interaction happens via grabbing.

jump.notes: Standard jump. Variable height. VERIFY exact values.

grab_system._note: THE CORE MECHANIC. Ristar's arms extend in the pressed direction to grab enemies and objects.

arm_extension.notes: Ristar extends arms in the held direction. Arms pass through enemies until grabbing one.

headbutt.description: After grabbing an enemy, pulling back and releasing swings Ristar forward into a headbutt.
notes: The grab-and-headbutt is the ONLY standard attack.
Enemy contact without grabbing does not damage the enemy — Ristar takes damage instead.
This inverts the typical platformer hitbox relationship: contact = you get hurt, not them.

grab_cancel.description: Grab with no target available — allows positioning or momentum manipulation.
grab_cancel.notes: Used in speedruns for movement optimization.

bounce_boost.description: Grab and bounce off an object diagonally behind you for a speed boost.

pole_swinging.description: Ristar can grab poles and swing around them in revolutions.

acceleration.description: Each full revolution around a pole increases Ristar's speed.

shooting_star_mode.description: Ristar enters 'Shooting Star Mode' — arms glow, launches at high velocity upon release.
shooting_star_mode.notes: Shooting Star Mode is the primary high-speed traversal technique in speedruns.

notes: Pole swing acceleration rewards patient, deliberate execution — the longer you spin, the faster you launch.
6.5 revolutions confirmed by TASVideos as the threshold for Shooting Star Mode.
Release angle from the pole determines launch direction.

ristar_standing.notes: Ristar is a round star character — hitbox likely circular or nearly so. VERIFY.

design_notes_for_giants_drink: The grab-and-headbutt system inverts the standard platformer combat model. Typically: player contact damages enemies. In Ristar: player contact damages you; you must grab, set up, and deliver the headbutt. This creates combat with a 'fishing' or 'wrestling' rhythm instead of the normal attack-and-dodge.

The pole swing acceleration mechanic creates a high-skill speedrun layer: patient players who spend extra time spinning poles can reach much higher launch velocities. This is 'windup' game design — the reward is proportional to the time invested in a single preparation action.

The grab arm's directionality (up/down/left/right) creates a combat vocabulary: horizontal grabs for most enemies, vertical grabs for elevated or low targets. Level design uses enemy placement to create situations where the grab direction is the key decision.

Ristar is a masterclass in using a single unique mechanic to differentiate a game. In 1995, the Genesis had many platformers — Ristar's grab system made it instantly distinct. The grab is used for combat, traversal (pole swinging), and puzzle solving.

The Shooting Star Mode (6 rotations → high-speed launch) gives skilled players a payoff that's both visually spectacular and mechanically rewarding. This is 'mastery payoff' design: the game rewards investment of setup time with a dramatic effect.
